title: Making Your Writing Come Alive with Precise Words
explanation_chunks: Great writers choose their words carefully, especially verbs – the action words! Instead of just saying 'the house woke up', they might use a more exciting word like 'alerted' to show a sudden, quick action.

Using precise verbs helps your reader picture exactly what's happening and makes your writing much more interesting. It's like choosing a super-specific colour instead of just 'red' – maybe 'crimson' or 'scarlet'!
We'll also practice creating our own science fiction house descriptions. Imagine what your dream futuristic house would do in the morning – what noises it makes, how it helps you get ready.

Don't forget those adverbial phrases! They are brilliant for adding extra detail about *when* or *how* things happen. Starting a sentence with one, like 'Room by room,' really grabs the reader's attention.
By combining precise verbs with vivid adverbial phrases, you can make your science fiction world feel incredibly real and exciting for anyone reading your story.
examples: Instead of 'walked', you could use 'strolled' or 'marched' to show *how* someone walked.
"Minute by minute, the robot brewed my tea." – 'Minute by minute' is an adverbial phrase telling us *when*.
key_points: Precise verbs make writing more engaging.
Adverbial phrases add detail about actions.
Practice planning and writing your own sci-fi descriptions.
Remember commas after introductory adverbial phrases.

vocabulary.definition: Action words that are very specific and detailed.
A book or tool that helps you find synonyms (words with similar meanings).
Groups of words that act like adverbs, giving more information about verbs.

explain_again: This page is all about making your stories sound super cool! We learn to pick the best action words, not just 'walked' but maybe 'strode' or 'crept'. And we add special phrases like 'Every morning,' to tell people *when* things happen in our imagined sci-fi houses!
fun_analogy: Picking precise verbs is like choosing the exact flavour of ice cream you want – not just 'sweet', but 'strawberry swirl' or 'mint chocolate chip'! It makes your sentence much more delicious and specific.
